input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Digital Twin provides real‑time monitoring of building subsystems such as HVAC, lighting, and structural health monitoring, predictive maintenance analytics, and optimisation of energy consumption. It offers direct control of actuators and equipment via an interface for Building Control Engineers, and immersive visualization dashboards accessible to Facility Managers. The twin supports target users – Building Control Engineers who configure and troubleshoot devices, Facility Managers who oversee day‑to‑day operations, and DT Engineers who extend system functionality through custom plugins or analytics. Capabilities include bidirectional data synchronization between the physical infrastructure and the Twin, event‑driven anomaly alerts, model‑based predictive algorithms, and a unified visualization platform built on enriched BIM data.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on a model‑driven deployment framework that extends BIM (IFC) models with cyber‑physical annotations for sensors, actuators, and controllers. Model‑driven engineering (MDE) extracts structured artefacts from the enriched BIM and automatically generates deployable Digital Twin components, minimizing manual coding. Real‑time data exchange is achieved via standard protocols such as MQTT or OPC‑UA to keep the physical and digital systems in continuous synchronization. Interactive visualization is enabled through a cloud‑based rendering engine integrated with the BIM, delivering immersive dashboards over web or AR interfaces. Custom services are added through well‑defined extension points exposed as micro‑service APIs, allowing DT Engineers to plug in analytics or domain‑specific functionality. The overall architecture is modular, maintainable, and supports end‑to‑end lifecycle integration from device modelling to operation.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Continuous real‑time bidirectional data exchange ensures the digital twin stays synchronised with its physical building counterpart, with runtime configuration allowing sub‑second update cycles where required; explicit numerical frequencies or latency thresholds are not specified in the source documents.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The architecture follows a centralised model where a single DT Framework hub orchestrates multiple digital twin modules (e.g., BIM enrichment, sensor ingestion, analytics, visualization) for each building; coordinating responsibilities are distributed among roles such as Building Control Engineers (device modelling and deployment), Facility Managers (monitoring and control), and DT Engineers (service extension), but no explicit decentralised twin instances are described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The DT constellation adopts a modular hub‑and‑spoke pattern: the DT Framework serves as the core hub facilitating bidirectional data exchange, while service components attach via standardised extension points; runtime configuration management permits dynamic reconfiguration without full redeployment, and resource management is implied through containerised, low‑overhead services. The system-wide coordination is achieved through the centralized architecture and the defined roles that contribute to the twin lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems relies on interoperability across heterogeneous platforms facilitated by standardised extension points and protocols. The framework’s non‑functional requirements emphasise interoperability and scalability, and the referenced open‑source frameworks (Eclipse Ditto, BaSyx, iTwin.js) provide connectivity and standard compliance, though specific protocols (e.g., MQTT, OPC UA) are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The framework enriches the IFC/BIM model with device metadata (e.g., Revit photometric values) and transforms it via a validation component, a conversion component, and a configuration component into a semantic representation that is consumed by the middleware stack (Eclipse Hono for at‑least‑once MQTT messaging, Eclipse Ditto for device registry and policy‑based validation) and by the UE 5 visualization engine. Geometric models preserve the original BIM 3‑D topology, behavioral models encode control logic defined by the Building Control Engineer, and analytical models represent device‑specific parameters (e.g., brightness, colour‑temperature limits). Data are persistently stored in Ditto’s event store; device configuration and state are managed by Ditto via policy enforcement; messaging follows an observable pattern implemented in the UnrealTwinController using UE 5 delegates. These models and data together form the digital counterpart of the physical building and provide the basis for interactive monitoring and control.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators are modeled in the enriched BIM and instantiated in the UE 5 scene by dedicated TwinComponents. A prominent example is the LightTwinComponent, which maps a physical lighting device to a UE 5 light. Commands such as 'on', 'brightness', and 'colour‑temperature (K)' are transmitted over MQTT via Hono to the DittoController and are validated against Ditto policy ranges (e.g. brightness 0–100 % and colour‑temperature 2700–6500 K). Pre‑execution safety checks in Ditto prevent conflicting or unsafe actuator states. The framework exposes a remote control interface that allows facility managers to issue commands through the UE 5 UI or programmatic APIs; the actual actuation is performed by the physical lighting hardware, whose behavior is governed by the control logic defined by the building engineer.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are represented as entities in the enriched BIM and their readings are streamed in real‑time to the twin via Eclipse Hono/MQTT and stored in Ditto’s event store for auditability. While the documents confirm the presence of multiple sensors (e.g., temperature, humidity, motion) and the use of a living lab deployment with an 22 m² office space, they do not provide concrete specifications such as sensor type, sampling frequency, accuracy, or transmission bandwidth. The framework supports high‑frequency, low‑latency data ingestion to keep the digital twin synchronized with the physical building.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is enforced through a dedicated validation component that checks compliance of enriched BIM data against architectural and semantic schemas. Eclipse Ditto’s policy‑based validation ensures that sensor values are within specified ranges and units, and that actuator commands satisfy safety constraints before execution. Fault tolerance is achieved by Hono’s at‑least‑once message delivery and Ditto’s persistent event storage, which guarantee data persistence across network failures. While explicit uncertainty quantification or error‑correction algorithms are not described, the use of policy validation, pre‑execution safety checks, and persistent event storage collectively provide a quality assurance process for models and data.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Real‑time data integration leverages Eclipse Hono as the connectivity gateway and Eclipse Ditto as the digital twin management broker. Physical IoT sensors publish measurements (e.g., temperature, occupancy, power consumption) via MQTT‑v5 or AMQP 1.0 messages that Hono routes to Ditto, which transforms them into JSON change events that update BIM‑derived digital twin representations. Triggering is event‑driven: a sensor’s telemetry packet flows whenever a new sample is acquired or a threshold is breached, and Hono’s schema‑validation and transformation pipelines ensure data quality before ingestion. Ditto’s Thing API maps the physical device identifiers to their corresponding virtual entities, enabling continuous bidirectional synchronization.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The framework employs Eclipse Hono to expose a broker‑agnostic connectivity layer supporting MQTT, AMQP 1.0, and CoAP, while Ditto provides the WebSocket/HTTP REST API for updating digital twin state. The network topology is a star architecture with edge gateways connecting to a central Hono service located at the corporate data centre; Ditto runs as a clustered micro‑service that persists JSON state. Bandwidth demands are modest (~200 bytes per telemetry packet) and latency tolerance extends to 200 ms due to Hono’s persistence queues. Redundancy is achieved via load‑balanced Hono clusters and Ditto’s sharding/replication features. Security is enforced through mutual TLS for device‑gateway communication and per‑device JWT tokens validated by Hono before message routing.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
1. Enriched BIM modelling: Building Control Engineers define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment, extending the IFC model with Building‑Control‑System components.
2. Model‑Driven Engineering extraction: The framework applies MDE principles to parse the enriched BIM models and generate structured semantic representations (e.g., JSON graphs or RDF) that capture device topologies and control logic.
3. Artifact generation and deployment: Automated transform engines produce deployable DT artefacts—typically containerised micro‑services or cloud‑based modules—requiring minimal manual configuration.
4. Runtime operation: The deployed digital twin synchronises real‑time data from physical buildings through bidirectional communication, exposing interactive visualization dashboards for monitoring and control.
5. Custom service integration: DT Engineers extend the twin by plugging in analytics, AI modules, or domain‑specific services via standardised extension points.
6. Lifecycle governance: Each phase is governed by automated scripts, versioned model artefacts and a modular architecture that supports rollback and traceability, ensuring consistent lifecycle management across the DT deployment.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process follows a Model‑Driven Architecture workflow: (a) Asset and device modelling in BIM; (b) Automatic extraction of machine‑readable models using MDE; (c) Generation of deployable DT artefacts and configuration of communication stacks (e.g., REST or WebSocket). Quality assurance is achieved by continuous real‑time data synchronisation that propagates any model changes to the live twin, providing built‑in consistency checks. Testing and validation are performed at the artefact level by deploying unit‑tests within the generated containers, and at runtime via simulation of sensor streams. Version control is implicit in the model extraction pipeline where each BIM revision yields a new DT artefact; evolution is managed through the modular extension framework that allows incremental replacement of services without redeploying the entire twin. The entire lifecycle is automated to minimise manual configuration and to support rapid iteration.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: At runtime the twin exposes an immersive, interactive visualization portal that allows Facility Managers to monitor system states, view sensor readings, and issue control commands. The modular architecture permits integration of analytics services (e.g., predictive maintenance models, energy‑usage optimisation algorithms) as plug‑in modules, enabling the generation of actionable insights based on real‑time data streams. Decision support flows through the Facility Manager’s interaction with the portal, where confidence in recommendations can be informed by the underlying analytics models; however the document does not specify explicit confidence metrics. The twin therefore supports data‑driven decision making by providing up‑to‑date telemetry and user‑configurable analytics pipelines.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The framework is built upon the BIM standard (Industry Foundation Classes – IFC) for asset representation and leverages Model‑Driven Engineering approaches that produce artefacts in open, structured formats. While specific certification requirements or formal compliance frameworks are not detailed, the modular architecture is designed to accommodate standard interoperability protocols (e.g., RESTful APIs, WebSocket) and to facilitate integration with existing AECO‑specific standards such as BIM. The document notes that many open‑source DT frameworks (e.g., Eclipse Ditto, BaSyx) provide robust connectivity and compliance with IoT standards, suggesting that similar adherence to standard communication protocols is anticipated, although not explicitly confirmed.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

message delivery and Ditto’s persistent event storage, ensuring resilience against network failures or service disruptions.
These mechanisms collectively maintain synchronization and
operational safety between physical assets and their DTs.

architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs

interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

_•_ **R2:** An automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention and enables reproducible DT setups ( **C2** ).

_•_ **R3:** Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and
Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical
assets and digital twins ( **C3** ).

_•_ **R4:** A modular architecture with defined extension points
to support scalable, service-oriented DT development in
AECO ( **C4** ).

Together, these contributions aim to operationalize scalable
and interoperable DTs in AECO contexts. By closing the gap
between design-time models and runtime operations, we offer
a sustainable path toward domain-wide DT adoption.
Our work is organized using Design Science Research
(DSR) [59] and provides artifacts to facilitate model-driven deployment of operational control DTs in the AECO domain. We
start with requirements elicitation (cf. Section III-A) and conclude with prototyping and experimentation (cf. Section V).



Our artifacts are developed in response to the following design
science problem [59]:


**Improve** _DT_ _deployment_ _in_ _BIM-based_ _infrastructure_
_projects_ (context)
**by designing** _a model-driven automation framework_ (artifact)
**that satisfies** _semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and_
_extensibility_ (requirement)
**in order to** _enable scalable DT engineering in the AECO_
_domain_ . (goal)


III. BIM- BASED D IGITAL T WINS

BIM is increasingly regarded as the principal modelling
paradigm in the AECO sector, providing a comprehensive
framework for generating, managing, and disseminating digital
building information throughout the entire building life cycle
with policy initiatives and regulatory mandates decisively
fostering and accelerating this development [60]. BIM enables
the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that
incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics
of building components and their relationships. These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design
decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance, and generate
documentation [61].
While there are also DTs and industry-specific solutions
for the digital mapping of assets in other industries, such
as the manufacturing industry (e.g. capitalizing on Modelbased Systems Engineering; MBSE) [1], [62], BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry [63],

Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
